title: Clause for concern? Super funds write AI into EBAs
full_text:
Two super funds charged with handling more than $500bn of Australian retirement savings have updated enterprise bargaining agreements to include AI-specific consultation clauses.
Aware Super’s EBA was approved by Fair Work earlier this month, and Australian Retirement Trust’s is still pending approval. They concede AI is a matter to be coded into industrial relations. How they intend to use the technology is unclear.
If the submitted variation is accepted, ART’s EBA clause is framed: “In the event that ART is seriously contemplating the adoption of an AI tool or feature which is reasonably assessed as likely to have a material impact by: completely automating an employee’s role; fundamentally changing the capability requirements of an employee’s role; or being used primarily to monitor, evaluate or make decisions about an employee’s work performance …” then it intends to consult and give “genuine consideration” to staff before making any decision.
Aware Super’s AI clause reads similarly. ART was contacted for comment but did not respond by deadline.
Financial Services Union national assistant secretary Nicole McPherson said the clauses were designed to help protect staff who, without the clause, could be at risk of losing their jobs to AI without realising the scope of the looming threat.
“By the time you’re tapped on the shoulder and told that your role is redundant or there’s a really significant change, it’s too late for you to be able to meaningfully re-skill or retrain into another role,” Ms McPherson said.
“At the moment AI is augmenting roles rather than replacing them. We aren’t seeing consultation on those augmentations. We’re just seeing that one day you come in, this new tool is available, and you are required to use it.
“Consultation has been bastardised … Consultation almost invariably leads to you losing your job. It’s just the departure lounge.”
Super Consumers Australia chief executive officer Xavier O’Halloran echoed the concerns, adding it was necessary for trustees to maintain standards despite the sugar rush of efficiencies and cost savings promised by AI.
“Having good principles in place is important no matter what the new tool is,” Mr O’Halloran said. “You don’t want AI to decide a life insurance or permanent disability insurance claim. It’s not something you can outsource to a computer. It’s similar with super.”
UNSW professor and superannuation law and governance expert Scott Donald said super funds were subject to extraordinary expectations compared to other financial institutions.
“There’s something special about the designation as ‘trustees’,” he said. “All the fiduciary elements of that, so there is a very deliberate tenor to being a trustee and one part of that is we expect our trustees to give a real and genuine consideration on behalf of members, which limits their ability to delegate.”
He added: “There are certainly places where AI can help, but you really need to be careful to ensure there’s a human in the loop somewhere, where the trustees can say we have engaged with that question properly and not put it into some black box to have an answer spat out.”
AI expert and UNSW AI Institute director Dr Sue Keay told The Australian it was important to regularly check super balances to maintain another level of protection.
A Colonial First State survey released earlier this year showed 83 per cent of Australians believe human interaction is essential for their finances, particularly in regards to super.
Fewer than 30 per cent of respondents said they were comfortable with AI being used for investment management.
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.
